content:
2 min read
Just now

--

One of the most expensive bugs in a payment system can be surprisingly simple:
A user clicks the payment button twice.
At first, this sounds harmless.
But in a real-world application, duplicate clicks can lead to:

• Multiple payment requests

• Duplicate orders

• User confusion

• Additional support tickets
While working on payment integrations, I learned that preventing duplicate actions requires both frontend and backend safeguards.
In this article, I’ll focus on what frontend developers can do.
The Problem
Imagine a standard payment button:
<button onClick={handlePayment}>
Pay Now
</button>Now imagine:

• User clicks “Pay Now”

• Network is slow

• Nothing appears to happen

• User clicks again
Without protection, two payment requests may be sent.
const handlePayment = async () => {
await initiatePayment();
};This looks fine.
Until users interact with it in the real world.
First Improvement: Disable the Button
The simplest solution is preventing repeated clicks.
const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);const handlePayment = async () => {
setLoading(true); try {
await initiatePayment();
} finally {
setLoading(false);
}
};<button
disabled={loading}
onClick={handlePayment}
>
{loading ? "Processing..." : "Pay Now"}
</button>This immediately improves the user experience.
But it’s still not enough.
The Race Condition Problem
React state updates are asynchronous.
Under certain circumstances, multiple clicks can occur before the UI updates.
This means:
setLoading(true);may not protect every edge case.
A safer approach is using a ref.
const processingRef = useRef(false);const handlePayment = async () => {
if (processingRef.current) return; processingRef.current = true; try {
await initiatePayment();
} finally {
processingRef.current = false;
}
};Now duplicate execution is blocked immediately.
Better User Feedback
Many duplicate actions happen because users think nothing is happening.
Instead of:
Pay Nowshow progress:
<button disabled={loading}>
{loading ? "Processing Payment..." : "Pay Now"}
</button>Even better:
Processing Payment...
Please do not refresh this page.Clear communication reduces unnecessary interactions.
Why Frontend Alone Isn’t Enough
This is the most important lesson I learned.
Frontend protection improves UX.
But it should never be the only protection.
Users can:

• Refresh the page

• Open multiple tabs

• Replay requests

• Use developer tools

• Trigger API calls manually
The backend must also support idempotency.
This means:
Multiple identical requests should produce a single result.
A common approach is using unique transaction identifiers:
{
"orderId": "ORD-12345",
"amount": 500
}If the same request arrives again, the backend recognizes it and prevents duplicate processing.
What This Taught Me
Before working on payment systems, I thought duplicate clicks were mostly a UI issue.
Now I see them as a system-design problem.
Reliable applications are built by thinking about:

• User behavior

• Network failures

• Retries

• Edge cases

• Data consistency
The most valuable engineering lessons often come from handling situations where things don’t go according to plan.
Final Thoughts
The next time you build a form submission, order flow, or payment screen, ask yourself:
“What happens if the user clicks this button five times?”
The answer will often reveal improvements you didn’t know your application needed.
And sometimes, preventing a second click can save much more than a few extra API requests.
